title: Dashboard shell uses hardcoded physical left/padding-left, so layout does not mirror in RTL (Arabic)
wcagCriterion: 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence (related); no direct SC for layout mirroring, but breaks the app's own documented RTL contract
description: globals.css states the intent explicitly: "RTL: every component is RTL-first; dir=\"rtl\" on <html> flips physical properties ... so layouts mirror automatically." DashboardShell does correctly toggle document.documentElement.dir/lang at runtime (dashboard-shell.tsx:35-36), but the shell's own layout uses hardcoded physical Tailwind classes instead of logical ones: the sidebar wrapper is pinned with `left-0` and the content column uses `md:pl-[278px]`. Neither responds to `dir="rtl"`.
expectedBehavior: When preferredLanguage is switched to Arabic (dir=rtl), the sidebar should move to the visual right edge and the main content column should reserve space on the right (e.g. via `inset-inline-start-0` / `ps-[278px]` logical utilities, or `[dir=rtl]:` overrides), matching the RTL-first design intent documented in globals.css.
actualBehavior: The sidebar stays pinned to the physical left of the viewport and main content keeps its left padding even after switching to Arabic, producing a left-docked nav in an otherwise right-to-left reading page.

evidence.snippet: <div className="fixed inset-y-0 left-0 z-40 hidden md:block">
<div className="min-h-screen md:pl-[278px]">
RTL: every component is RTL-first; dir="rtl" on <html> flips physical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-inline-end) so layouts mirror automatically.

reproduction: 1. Sign in to the dashboard. 2. Click the language toggle (Globe icon) in the header to switch to العربية. 3. Observe the sidebar remains on the left and main content padding is unchanged, rather than mirroring to the right.
technicalRisk: Layout primitives don't use Tailwind logical properties (ps-/pe-/start-/end-), so the RTL contract only holds for components authored after this pattern was adopted, not the shell itself.
businessRisk: Arabic-speaking users (the primary market — Saudi Arabia) get a visually inconsistent, unpolished experience on every single screen, undermining the product's "Saudi-luxury" positioning and potentially eroding trust in a govt/enterprise HR context.
recommendation: Replace `left-0`/`pl-[278px]` (and similar physical positioning in header.tsx, employees list search icon, etc.) with Tailwind logical-property utilities (`start-0`, `ps-[278px]`) or add `[dir=rtl]:right-0 [dir=rtl]:pr-[278px]` overrides, then visually QA every dashboard page in Arabic.
workaround: None for end users; Arabic users can still complete tasks, just with a mirrored-incorrectly nav rail.

title: Language toggle on the login page is non-functional (dead button)
wcagCriterion: N/A (UX/Nielsen: user control and freedom; consistency and standards)
description: The login page renders what looks like a working EN/Arabic toggle in the top bar, but the "العربية" element is a `<button>` with no `onClick` handler at all, and "EN" is a plain `<span>`, not an interactive control. Every other authenticated page in the app has a working language toggle (header.tsx uses `setPreferredLanguage`), so this looks like an oversight specific to the pre-login screen.
expectedBehavior: Clicking "العربية" switches the login page (and ideally the whole pre-auth flow) to Arabic/RTL, consistent with the working toggle used everywhere post-login.
actualBehavior: Clicking the "العربية" button does nothing; there is no state, no dir change, and no visual feedback of any kind.

evidence.snippet:
<button type="button" className="rounded-full px-3 py-1 transition hover:bg-white/10 hover:text-white">
  العربية
</button>
EN

reproduction: 1. Visit /login while signed out. 2. Click "العربية" in the top-right corner. 3. Nothing happens — page stays in English/LTR.
technicalRisk: None beyond dead code; low complexity fix (wire to the same Lang state pattern used in regulatory-context.tsx).
businessRisk: First impression for Arabic-speaking prospects/employees is a broken control before they even reach the product — particularly damaging for a Saudi-market SaaS whose demo/sales narrative leans on bilingual polish.
recommendation: Wire the login-page toggle to the same `Lang`/dir-switching mechanism used in DashboardShell, or at minimum route users to a `?lang=ar` variant of the login form with translated copy.
workaround: Users can complete login in English and switch language after signing in.

title: Form labels not programmatically associated with inputs (missing htmlFor/id) across core HR forms
role: HR Manager, Employee, Department Manager, Super Admin
wcagCriterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A), 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A)
description: Several of the app's oldest/core forms render plain `<label>` text with no `htmlFor`, paired with `<Input>`/`<select>` elements that have no `id`. This breaks the programmatic label-control relationship: clicking the label text does not focus the field, and screen readers announce the field with no accessible name (or fall back to the placeholder only). By contrast, newer forms (login, signup, recruitment/*, retention/*, offboarding/*, documents/upload) consistently pair `htmlFor` with matching `id`s, showing the pattern is known and used elsewhere in the codebase — it just wasn't applied to these forms.
expectedBehavior: Every `<label>` should carry `htmlFor` matching the paired input's `id` (or wrap the input), so assistive technology announces a clear field name and label-click focuses the control — as already done correctly in apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx.
actualBehavior: Labels and inputs are visually adjacent but have no id/htmlFor link in the forms listed in Evidence.

evidence.snippet:
<label className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-700">Full Name *</label>
<Input value={fullName} onChange={(e) => setFullName(e.target.value)} placeholder="Employee full name" />
{currentEmployee?.fullName ?? "Loading..."}

<label htmlFor="email" className="block text-sm font-medium text-slate-700">Email</label>
<input id="email" ... />

reproduction: 1. Open /employees/new, step to the form. 2. Inspect any field's label in devtools — no `for` attribute; input has no `id`. 3. With a screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver), tab into 'Full Name' — it is announced generically rather than as 'Full Name, edit text'.
technicalRisk: Low complexity, high fan-out: the same unlabeled pattern repeats across ~9 fields per form in at least 11 files.
businessRisk: Blocks or degrades task completion for screen-reader users (employees self-onboarding, HR staff with visual impairments) on some of the most business-critical forms in the product (new hire creation, leave requests, payroll setup).
recommendation: Add matching `id`/`htmlFor` pairs to all fields in the listed forms (or introduce a small `<FormField>` wrapper component in packages/ui that auto-generates and wires ids, then migrate these pages to it) to prevent regression.
workaround: Sighted mouse/touch users are unaffected; only assistive-technology users are impacted.

title: Required-field markers are visual-only and validation errors are not announced to assistive technology
description: Required fields are marked with a plain "*" in the label text, but the underlying ``/`
expectedBehavior: Required inputs should carry `required`/`aria-required="true"`; the error summary should be in a `role="alert"` (or `aria-live="assertive"`) container so screen-reader users are told immediately when submission fails, matching apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx:78-85.
actualBehavior: Submitting either form with missing/invalid data updates a plain div with the error text; a screen-reader user who has already moved focus away from the form is not notified anything happened.

evidence.snippet:
{error &&

{error}

}

reproduction: 1. Open /leave/new, submit with no dates selected. 2. Error text appears visually but a screen reader user focused elsewhere gets no announcement.
technicalRisk: Low — add `role="alert"` to the existing conditional div/p, and `required` to inputs already tracked as mandatory in the submit handler's validation.
businessRisk: Screen-reader users may repeatedly submit a failing form without understanding why, especially self-service employees filing leave requests unassisted.
recommendation: Add `role="alert"` to error containers and `required`/`aria-required` to fields already validated as mandatory in handleSubmit.
workaround: Sighted users see the red banner directly below the button they just clicked.

title: Focus outline removed with no replacement on Super Admin tenant filter input
description: Across the codebase, every other instance of `focus:outline-none` is paired with a replacement indicator (`focus:ring-2`, `focus:border-*`, etc. — 28 of 29 occurrences checked). The tenant filter input on the Super Admin page is the sole exception: it removes the native outline and supplies no visible focus state at all.
expectedBehavior: Keyboard users tabbing to the filter input should see a visible focus indicator (ring/border/box-shadow), consistent with every other input in the app.
actualBehavior: Tabbing into the field shows no visible change; the input is indistinguishable from its unfocused state.

evidence.snippet: className="w-56 bg-transparent text-sm text-slate-800 placeholder:text-slate-400 focus:outline-none"

reproduction: 1. Open /super-admin as a super_admin user. 2. Tab through the page until focus reaches the 'Filter by name, CR, schema' input. 3. No focus ring or border change is visible.
businessRisk: Keyboard-only super-admin operators (a small but high-privilege user group) can lose track of focus position on a page that controls tenant-level data.
recommendation: Add `focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-600` (or equivalent) matching the sibling filter/search inputs elsewhere in the app.

title: Icon-only "clear search" button has no accessible name
description: The small 'X' button that clears the tenant filter text renders only a lucide `<X>` icon with no `aria-label`, so it has no accessible name.
expectedBehavior: The button should expose an accessible name, e.g. `aria-label="Clear filter"`.
actualBehavior: Screen readers announce it as an unlabeled 'button'.

evidence.snippet:
<button onClick={() => setSearch("")} className="rounded-full p-0.5 text-slate-400 hover:text-slate-700">
  <X className="h-3 w-3" />
</button>

reproduction: Type text into the tenant filter, tab to the clear (X) button, listen with a screen reader — it announces only 'button'.
workaround: Sighted users can infer purpose from the icon.

title: Hand-rolled "Save Map View" modal lacks dialog semantics, focus trap and Escape-to-close
wcagCriterion: 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (A) / 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A)
description: The app already ships an accessible modal primitive (packages/ui/src/ui/dialog.tsx, built on @radix-ui/react-dialog, with built-in focus trap, Escape handling and aria-modal) and uses it correctly elsewhere (apps/web/app/(dashboard)/attendance/portal/page.tsx:249-328). The Guide Map page's "Save This View" flow instead renders a plain `fixed inset-0` overlay `<div>` with no `role="dialog"`, no `aria-modal="true"`, no focus trap, and no keydown handler for Escape.
expectedBehavior: Opening 'Save This View' should trap focus inside the dialog, be announced as a dialog to screen readers, and close on Escape — matching the Punch Dialog pattern on /attendance/portal.
actualBehavior: Tab can move focus out of the overlay into the page behind it; Escape does nothing; no dialog role is exposed.

evidence.snippet:
{showSaveDialog && (
  <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/50">
    <Card className="w-96">

<Dialog open={!!selectedEmployee} onOpenChange={() => setSelectedEmployee(null)}>
  <DialogContent className="max-w-lg">

reproduction: 1. Open /attendance/guide-map. 2. Click 'Save This View'. 3. Press Tab repeatedly — focus escapes into the sidebar/header behind the overlay. 4. Press Escape — nothing happens.
technicalRisk: Low — swap the raw div for the existing `Dialog`/`DialogContent` components already imported elsewhere in the same file's sibling pages.
businessRisk: Keyboard/screen-reader users saving a guide map location can get lost behind the overlay or be unable to dismiss it without a mouse.
recommendation: Replace the plain overlay with `Dialog`/`DialogContent` from @hrms-app/ui, consistent with attendance/portal/page.tsx.
workaround: Mouse/touch users can click Cancel or click outside conceptually (though click-outside isn't wired either, since it's not a real dialog).

title: In-app help chat has no aria-live region for new messages
description: The floating Taazur Help chat appends new assistant/user messages to a scrolling `<div>` with no `aria-live` attribute. A screen-reader user who submits a question and then reviews other parts of the page will not be notified when the AI's reply arrives.
expectedBehavior: The message list (or a visually-hidden companion region) should be `aria-live="polite"` so new assistant replies are announced automatically, similar to how apps/web/app/(dashboard)/loading.tsx correctly uses `aria-live="polite"` for its skeleton state.
actualBehavior: New messages (including the 'Thinking…' pending state) are silently inserted with no announcement.

evidence.snippet:
<div ref={scrollRef} className="flex-1 space-y-2.5 overflow-y-auto p-3 text-sm">
  {messages.map((m) => (

reproduction: 1. Open the chat widget (bottom-right sparkle button). 2. Ask a question. 3. With a screen reader running, the reply appearing in the transcript is never announced.
technicalRisk: Low — add `aria-live="polite" aria-relevant="additions"` to the scroll container, or a visually-hidden live region announcing the latest assistant message.
businessRisk: The help chatbot is the app's self-service support channel; screen-reader users get a degraded, non-functional experience from the very feature meant to assist them.
workaround: Users can manually re-focus/re-read the panel after sending a message.

title: Destructive delete actions with zero confirmation step
description: Two delete actions fire their mutation immediately on click with no confirmation of any kind — not even a native `confirm()`. This is inconsistent with the rest of the app, which guards comparable destructive actions (delete department, delete document, delete policy, revoke invite, cancel expense) behind a `window.confirm(...)` prompt.
expectedBehavior: Deleting a saved guide map or a job requisition should require an explicit confirmation step before the mutation fires, matching the pattern used for departments/documents/policies elsewhere in the app.
actualBehavior: A single click on the trash icon (guide map) or the 'Delete' button (job requisition) immediately deletes the record.

evidence.snippet:
<Button size="icon" variant="ghost" className="h-7 w-7 shrink-0 text-muted-foreground hover:text-red-500" onClick={() => deleteMutation.mutate(gm.id)}>
  <Trash2 className="h-3.5 w-3.5" />
</Button>

<Button variant="destructive" onClick={() => deleteMutation.mutate(job.id)}>
  Delete
</Button>
if (confirm("Delete this department?")) deleteMutation.mutate(node.id);

reproduction: 1. Open /attendance/guide-map with at least one saved map. 2. Click the trash icon next to it. 3. The map is deleted immediately, no prompt. Similarly for a draft job requisition's Delete button on /recruitment/jobs/[id].
technicalRisk: Low — add the same `confirm(...)` guard (or, better, a themed AlertDialog) used elsewhere in the codebase.
businessRisk: A single misclick permanently deletes a saved guide map or a job requisition with no recovery path visible in the UI, risking accidental data loss for HR/recruiting workflows.
recommendation: Add a confirmation step (ideally a themed AlertDialog rather than native confirm — see UX-010) before both mutations.
workaround: None; deletion is immediate and irreversible from the UI.

title: Destructive-action confirmations rely on unstyled native window.confirm() instead of the app's own dialog
description: Six separate destructive flows use the browser-native, unstyled `confirm()` dialog rather than the app's own themed Dialog component (already available in packages/ui and used for the Punch Dialog on /attendance/portal). Native `confirm()` cannot be localized to match the user's chosen Arabic/English preference, doesn't match the Saudi-luxury visual language, and blocks the JS main thread.
expectedBehavior: Destructive confirmations should use the shared Dialog/AlertDialog pattern, with buttons/copy that respect `preferredLanguage`.
actualBehavior: Confirmations show the browser's default OK/Cancel dialog with English-only, unstyled text regardless of the app's current language setting.

evidence.snippet: if (confirm("Revoke this invitation?")) revokeInvite.mutate({ inviteId: id });

if (confirm(`Delete "${p.name}"?`)) {
  deleteMutation.mutate(p.id);
onClick={() => { if (confirm("Cancel this expense?")) cancel.mutate(row.id); }}

reproduction: Switch the app to Arabic, then trigger any of the listed delete/cancel/revoke actions — the confirmation dialog remains in English with no theming.
technicalRisk: Low functional risk; purely a consistency/i18n gap.
businessRisk: Minor brand/consistency erosion; not a blocker to task completion.
recommendation: Introduce a shared `useConfirm()`/`ConfirmDialog` built on the existing Dialog primitive and migrate these six call sites to it.

title: No "skip to main content" link before the sidebar navigation
description: There is no skip link anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via repo-wide search). Every dashboard page places roughly 15-20 sidebar navigation links (Sidebar's `<nav>`, apps/web/components/sidebar.tsx:158-184) before the `<main>` landmark in the DOM order, with no mechanism for keyboard/screen-reader users to jump directly to page content.
expectedBehavior: A visually-hidden-until-focused 'Skip to main content' link should be the first focusable element, targeting the `<main>` element in dashboard-shell.tsx:60.
actualBehavior: Keyboard users must tab through the entire sidebar nav (and header controls) on every single page load/navigation before reaching page content.

evidence.snippet:
<Header user={user} onOpenMenu={() => setMobileOpen(true)} />
<main className="mx-auto w-full max-w-[1680px] p-4 sm:p-6 lg:p-8">{children}</main>
<nav className="flex-1 overflow-y-auto px-3 pb-4 ...">

reproduction: Load any dashboard page and press Tab from the top — the first ~20 stops are sidebar links, not page content.
businessRisk: Adds friction for keyboard-only and screen-reader users on every navigation, especially painful for people who use the app throughout the workday (e.g. HR staff processing many records).
recommendation: Add a standard skip link as the first child of DashboardShell's root, pointing to an `id` on the `<main>` element.

title: Shared Table component's header cells omit explicit scope="col"
page: All tables (Employees, Documents, Compliance, Qiwa, Payroll, Retention, etc.)
description: The shared `TableHead` renders a bare `<th>` with no `scope` attribute. Most browsers/screen readers infer `scope="col"` for `<th>` inside a `<thead>`, so this is not a hard failure, but explicit scope is recommended best practice, particularly for the app's data-dense HR tables which are otherwise the most tabular-heavy content in the product.
expectedBehavior: `TableHead` should default to `scope="col"` (with an override prop for any future row-header use).
reproduction: Inspect any TableHead render (e.g. /employees) — the `<th>` has no `scope` attribute.
technicalRisk: Trivial, single shared-component change benefits every table in the app.
businessRisk: Minor; most screen readers already infer column headers correctly.
recommendation: Default `scope="col"` in TableHead, consumed once by all ~15+ tables in the app.
workaround: None needed; most AT infers this correctly already.

title: Table rows use role="link" plus a nested real <button>, an unconventional interactive pattern
wcagCriterion: 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — best-practice deviation, not a hard failure
description: Each employee `<tr>` is given `role="link"`, `tabIndex={0}`, an `aria-label`, and both click and Enter/Space keydown handlers to navigate to the employee's profile — genuinely more accessible than a typical unlabeled div-onClick pattern, and this is the only such pattern found in a repo-wide scan for onClick divs (none found). However, `role="link"` on a `<tr>` that performs a client-side `router.push` (not a real href) is non-standard — links normally support right-click/open-in-new-tab and show a URL on hover/status bar, neither of which this provides. The row also contains a real nested `<Button>` ("View") that duplicates the row's own action, i.e. two overlapping interactive targets on one row.
expectedBehavior: Either wrap the row's primary cell content in a real `<a href="/employees/{id}">` (progressively enhanced with client routing) so link semantics are accurate, or use `role="button"` if it must stay a synthetic control — and drop the redundant nested 'View' button once the row itself is fully operable.
actualBehavior: Row exposes `role="link"` without real link affordances, plus a redundant nested button performing the same navigation.

evidence.snippet:
<TableRow
  key={employee.id}
  className="cursor-pointer focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-700/30"
  role="link"
  tabIndex={0}
  aria-label={`Open ${employee.fullName}`}
  onClick={() => router.push(`/employees/${employee.id}`)}

<Button variant="ghost" size="sm" onClick={(e) => { e.stopPropagation(); router.push(`/employees/${employee.id}`); }}>
  View
</Button>

reproduction: Inspect any employee row's accessibility tree — role is 'link' but there is no href; a nested button with the same destination sits inside it.
technicalRisk: Low; functionally keyboard-operable today (Enter/Space both handled), so this is a refinement rather than a blocker.
businessRisk: Minor confusion for screen-reader users encountering a 'link' that behaves like a button, and slightly redundant navigation model.
recommendation: Consider using `role="button"` (since it triggers a client-side action, not a navigable href) and drop the duplicate 'View' button, or convert the whole row to a real anchor for proper link semantics.
workaround: Row is already keyboard operable via Enter/Space; not blocking.
